1: qôlî ʾel-ʾĕlōhîm wĕʾeṣʿāqāh — I cried out to God with my full voice. The cohortative ʾeṣʿāqāh (I will/let me cry out) — not a quiet prayer but a loud, urgent cry. Ṣāʿaq (to cry out) is the word used of Israel's cry from Egypt (Exod 2:23; 3:7) — the Exodus register is activated from the psalm's opening verse. qôlî ʾel-ʾĕlōhîm wĕhaʾăzîn ʾēlāy — I cried to God, and he listened. The hiphil of ʾāzan (to give ear, to listen attentively) — God inclined his ear. The second half of v1 already contains the resolution the body of the psalm struggles to reach.
2: bĕyôm ṣārātî ʾădōnāy dārāshtî — in the day of my trouble I sought the Lord. Dārash (to seek, to inquire — the same verb of seeking God at a sanctuary or oracle) — distress drives purposeful seeking. yādî laylāh niggĕrāh wĕlōʾ tāpûg — through the night my hand was outstretched without ceasing. Nāgar (to flow out, be poured out — used of water) — the hand stretched out in prayer, poured out through the night without flagging. māʾănāh hinnāḥēm nafshî — my soul refused to be comforted. The niphal of nāḥam (to be comforted) refused — a deliberate rejection of lesser comforts, holding out for God alone, which paradoxically prolongs the distress.
3: ʾezkĕrāh ʾĕlōhîm wĕʾehĕmāyāh — I thought of God and I groaned. The paradox: remembering God at this stage produces groaning rather than comfort (the therapeutic remembering of vv11-20 comes later, after the crisis reaches its lowest point). Hāmāh (to murmur, roar, groan — used of the churning sea and of tumultuous crowds) — the inner noise of unresolved distress. ʾāśîḥāh wĕtiʿaṭṭēph rûḥî selāh — I poured out my grief until my spirit grew faint. ʿāṭaph (hithpael: to wrap oneself up, to grow faint from exhaustion) — the spirit collapsing under unresolved grief.
4: ʾāḥaztā shĕmurôt ʿênāy — you kept my eyes wide open through the night. The subject shifts to God: ʾāḥaztā (you held, you grasped — from ʾāḥaz: to seize, take hold) — God himself holding the eyelids open. The insomnia is not merely psychological but divinely caused: God is keeping the Psalmist awake. This makes God the agent of the very suffering the Psalmist brings to him in complaint — the same move as Ps 73:14 (God who afflicts his own). niphʿamtî wĕlōʾ ʾădabbēr — I was too troubled to speak. Pāʿam (to beat, trouble, agitate — used of Pharaoh's troubled spirit, Gen 41:8) — agitated beyond articulation.
5: ḥishshabtî yāmîm miqqedem — I think about the days of old. Ḥāshab (to reckon, compute, think deliberately — the accountant's word) — purposeful recollection rather than nostalgic wandering. shĕnôt ʿôlāmîm — the years of long ago. ʿôlām (hidden, of long ago, ancient) — the years whose significance requires excavation, the buried history of divine faithfulness. The movement from personal crisis (vv1-4) toward historical memory (vv5-6, then vv11-20) begins here — the therapeutic arc of the psalm.
6: ʾezkĕrāh nĕgînātî ballāylāh — I recall my song in the night. Nĕgînāh (song, melody — from nāgan: to play a stringed instrument) — the Psalmist recalls his former worship song, the night song that once came naturally and now does not. ʿim-lĕbābî ʾāśîḥāh — I ponder in my heart. Śîaḥ (to muse, meditate — the same root as the meditation of Ps 119:15,23) — deliberate inward rumination. wîḥaqqēr rûḥî — my spirit searches deeply. Ḥāqar (to search out, probe — used of thorough legal investigation) — the spirit conducting a thorough examination of its own condition.
7: hălĕʿôlāmîm yiznaḥ ʾădōnāy — will the Lord reject us forever? Zānaḥ (to reject, push away — the same verb as Ps 74:1; 44:9) — the communal lament vocabulary applied to personal experience. The series of questions in vv7-9 form a theodicy catechism, each attacking a specific divine attribute: permanence (v7), faithfulness in steadfast love (v8), grace and compassion (v9). wĕlōʾ-yōsîph lirṣôt ʿôd — will he never again be pleased with us? Rāṣāh (to be pleased, accept favorably — the technical word for divine acceptance of a sacrifice, Lev 1:4) — will divine acceptance never return?
8: heʾāphes lāneṣaḥ ḥasdô — has his steadfast love ceased once and for all? Ḥesed (steadfast love, covenant loyalty) — the foundational divine attribute of Exod 34:6-7. Āphēs (to cease, come to an end — used of armies that cease, of hope that ends) — the question is not whether God can cease to love but whether the silence is permanent. gāmar ʾōmer lĕdōr wādōr — has his promise failed for every generation to come? ʾŌmer (word, promise, speech) — the divine word that was the foundation of covenant confidence. Has the word gone silent for all future generations?
9: hăshākah ḥannôt ʾêl — has God forgotten to show grace? Ḥānan (to show grace, to be gracious — the divine attribute proclaimed at Sinai, Exod 34:6: ḥannûn wĕraḥûm) — the question strips the attribute from God and holds it up as a challenge. ʾim-qāphāṣ bĕʾap raḥămāyw selāh — has he locked away his compassion in his anger? Qāphaṣ (to shut, close — a rare verb suggesting a snapping shut) — the raḥămîm (compassions/mercies, from reḥem: womb, the maternal depths) clamped shut by anger. The selah marks the nadir of the questioning.
10: waʾōmar ḥallôtî hîʾ — then I said: this is the sickness that is killing me. The most exegetically contested verse in the psalm. Ḥallôtî (my sickness, my wounding — from ḥālāh: to be sick, wounded) — the Psalmist names his condition. shĕnôt yĕmîn ʿelyôn — the years of the right hand of the Most High. Two main readings: (1) the right hand of the Most High has changed — the crisis thought that God's saving power has withdrawn; (2) the years of the Most High's right hand are past — the golden years of divine action seem over. Either reading makes v10 the pivot: the crisis-thought about God's apparent withdrawal is immediately followed by I will remember (v11). The Psalmist does not resolve the crisis by argument but by therapeutic memory of the Exodus.
11: ʾezkōr maʿallĕlê-YAH — I will call to mind the works of the LORD. Maʿallēl (deed, work — from ʿālal: to act, to deal with) — the specific, concrete acts of God in history. The name YAH (the shortened form of YHWH, found especially in the Psalter and in hallĕlûyāh) — personal, intimate. kî-ʾezkĕrāh miqqedem pilelĕkhā — yes, I will remember your wonders from of old. The cohortative ʾezkĕrāh (I will remember) is a deliberate act of will against the current despair — the Psalmist chooses to move from questioning (vv7-9) to remembering.
12: wĕhāgîtî bĕkhol-poʿlekā — I will meditate on all your works. Hāgāh (to murmur, meditate — Ps 1:2's word for sustained Torah meditation) — the same contemplative practice turned from individual crisis toward historical acts of God. ûbĕʿălîlôtekhā ʾāśîḥāh — and think carefully about everything you have done. The ʾāśîḥāh (meditative contemplation — the same word used of distress in v3) is now redirected: the interior groaning becomes productive meditation on redemptive history. Memory is the therapy.
13: ʾĕlōhîm baqqōdesh darkekā — O God, your path runs through what is holy. Baqqōdesh darkekā — the divine way is through the holy place; the sanctuary is where God's way is made known. The sanctuary as the place of divine revelation recurs from Ps 73:17 — it is where the eschatological perspective becomes visible, where crisis is met with memory. mî-ʾêl gādôl kĕʾĕlōhîm — what god anywhere is as great as our God? The incomparability formula — no divine competitor approaches YHWH in the acts now about to be rehearsed.
14: ʾattāh hāʾêl ʿōśēh pheleʾ — you are the God who performs wonders. Pheleʾ (wonder, extraordinary deed — Exod 15:11 in the Song of the Sea: nôrāʾ tĕhillōt ʿōśēh pheleʾ, awesome in praises, working wonders). The Exodus doxology vocabulary begins consciously here — the Psalmist is entering the Song of the Sea tradition. hôdaʿtā bāʿammîm ʿuzzekā — you have declared your power among the nations. The Exodus was a public, international event — a display before all peoples (cf. Exod 9:16; Josh 2:10), not a private national experience.
15: gāʾaltā bizzĕrôaʿ ʿammekā — with your strong arm you redeemed your people. Gāʾal (kinsman-redemption) + zĕrôaʿ (the arm, the outstretched arm) — the full Exodus formula compressed into four Hebrew words. The combination recalls the Deuteronomic summaries (Deut 5:15; 26:8). bĕnê-yaʿăqōb wĕyôsēph selāh — the descendants of Jacob and Joseph. The unusual dual mention of Jacob and Joseph — perhaps including both southern and northern traditions (Joseph = Ephraim/Manasseh), embracing the whole people in the Exodus memory.
16: rāʾûkhā mayim ʾĕlōhîm rāʾûkhā mayim yāḥîlû — the waters saw you, O God; the waters saw you and recoiled. Rāʾāh (to see) repeated — the waters as sentient witnesses to the divine presence. Yāḥîlû (they writhed, were in anguish — from ḥûl: to writhe as in labor-pain) — the Red Sea experiencing the anguish of a woman in childbirth at God's approach. ʾaf yirgezu tĕhōmôt — the very depths were shaken. Tĕhôm (the deep, the primordial ocean — Gen 1:2) trembling before the Creator — the Exodus crossing narrated in creation-battle terms.
17: zōrĕmû mayim ʿābôt — the clouds poured down rain. Zāram (to pour out in a torrent — violent, sudden rain) — the storm-theophany begins. qôl nātĕnû shĕḥāqîm — the sky resounded. Shĕḥāqîm (the thin upper air — from shāḥaq: to grind fine) — the upper atmosphere resonating with the divine voice. ʾaf-ḥăṣāṣêkhā yithallākû — your arrows flashed out in every direction. Ḥēṣ (arrow) — divine lightning as divine arrows, combining the warrior and storm images in one theophany.
18: qôl raʿămĕkhā baggalgal — your thunder rolled through the churning heavens. Galgal (wheel, whirlwind, rolling thing — the wheel-sound of thunder rolling across the vault of heaven). hēʾîrû bĕrāqîm tēbēl — your lightning illuminated the world. Tēbēl (the inhabited earth — the whole world with its peoples) lit up by divine lightning. rāgĕzāh wattiʿash hāʾāreṣ — the earth trembled and quaked. The two earthquake verbs (rāgaz: to quake with agitation; raʿash: to tremble violently) combine Sinai vocabulary (Exod 19:18) with the Exodus crossing into one cosmic event.
19: bayyām darkekā ûshbîlekhā bĕmayim rabbîm — your path went through the sea, your way through the great deep. The Exodus crossing narrated as divine theophany — God's way passes through the sea, through the primordial chaos-waters (mayim rabbîm: the great waters, Ps 29:3). wĕʿiqbôtekhā lōʾ nōdāʿû — but your footprints could not be found. ʿĀqēb (heel, footprint, track) — God left no traceable footprint in the sea. The divine transcendence expressed as invisibility: God acts decisively in history, parts the sea, leads the people — and leaves no trace that can be pinned down, analyzed, or reduced to a natural phenomenon. He cannot be controlled.
20: nāḥîtā kattsōʾn ʿammekā — you guided your people like a flock. Nāḥāh (to lead, guide — specifically of divine leading in the wilderness, Exod 13:21; 15:13; Deut 32:12) + ṣōʾn (flock) — the shepherd image closing the Exodus retrospective. bĕyad-mōsheh wĕʾahărōn — through the leadership of Moses and Aaron. The psalm ends not with triumphant theophany but with human instruments of divine guidance. The personal crisis of vv1-9 is answered not with a direct divine address but with the reminder that God has always led through human intermediaries. The Psalmist stands in that tradition — the God who led then continues to lead now.
